title: WHOOP Decoded: A Year of Recovery Data
text:
WHOOP Decoded

What does your recovery data actually tell you?



365


Days Tracked




81 ms


Avg HRV




53%


Avg Recovery




33%


Days in the Green




447


Workouts Logged










HRV — heart rate variability is the variation between heartbeats in milliseconds. A high HRV means your body is calm and ready for exercise. A low HRV means your body is still dealing with stress, hard training, or poor sleep.




Why it matters: WHOOP gives you a recovery number, but HRV is the significant figure underneath it. Watching the trend over months reveals your fitness habits.




Use the range slider at the bottom to zoom into any stretch of weeks. Hover any dot to see the full breakdown for that day. The white line shows your 7-day rolling average — the true signal beneath the daily noise.




Patterns indicate consistencies or inconsistencies in training and recovery. Dips can be seen after harder training blocks or busier times of the year. Spikes can be seen after rest.





Chart 1 · Your HRV Over a Year
















WHOOP says sleep drives recovery. But which part of sleep? Total hours? Efficiency? How much deep or REM sleep you got?




Use the dropdown to swap the x-axis between different sleep metrics. A tight upward cluster means that metric predicts your recovery. A scattered blob means it does not.




Common finding: Sleep performance % and sleep debt tend to be stronger predictors than raw hours — it is not just about quantity.




Each dot is one day. The color still shows your recovery zone — green dots should cluster toward the top right.





Chart 2 · Which sleep metric actually predicts recovery?
















Here is what WHOOP does not tell you directly: your HRV that morning predicts how hard a workout will feel. When HRV is low, your heart beats faster to produce the same output — your easy run suddenly feels hard.




What to look for: A downward trend — higher HRV on the x-axis should mean lower average heart rate on the y-axis. If the pattern holds for you, low-HRV days are days to go easy.




Use the activity buttons to filter by sport. Running and Jiu Jitsu are the most revealing — cardio sports where heart rate directly measures effort. On low-HRV days, the same pace requires a meaningfully higher heart rate.




The pattern is clearest in Running — filter to it and watch how the dots slope downward from left to right. Higher HRV mornings consistently produce lower workout heart rates at the same effort level.





Chart 3 · The insight WHOOP never surfaces
















Pick any variable for X and Y — every dot is one day, colored by recovery zone.




Try HRV vs. Resting HR — these two move in opposite directions when your body is stressed. Or Sleep Debt vs. Recovery to see how cumulative sleep loss compounds over time.




Where to start: Set X to Sleep Debt and Y to HRV. A downward slope means the longer your debt builds, the harder your nervous system works to recover.




Green dots should cluster toward the top-right of any meaningful pairing. Scattered dots mean those two variables do not predict each other — that is useful information too.





Chart 4 · Discover Trends
















Every square is one day. Green means your body was ready. Red means it was not. Yellow is in between.




What to look for: Streaks of red often follow heavy training blocks or poor sleep weeks. Green clusters usually mean you backed off at the right time.




Scroll back through the year — patterns in the calendar reveal things the line chart buries. A bad month looks very different from a bad week. Hover any square to see the full breakdown for that day.





Chart 5 · 365 Days at a Glance

















WHOOP tells you your recovery score after you wake up. This tool works the other way — enter what your body looked like today, and it tells you exactly how many hours to sleep tonight to wake up in the Green.




How it works: A model trained on your 365 days solves backwards from your recovery target. Given your HRV and strain, it calculates the hours of sleep needed to hit each zone.




Set your HRV from this morning and today’s strain. The output is a concrete sleep target — not a percentage, but hours and minutes.




This is what WHOOP never tells you.





Chart 6 · What Do I Need Tonight?


This Morning HRV (ms)81
Today Strain14.0
Hours of Sleep Needed Tonight

Wake up Green (67%+ recovery)8h 12m
Stay Yellow (34%+ recovery)3h 50m

With HRV 81 and strain 14, sleep 8h 12m tonight to wake up Green.
